name: Colorado SB 26-189 (Automated Decision-Making Technology)
description: Colorado law establishing consumer notice, disclosure, and human-review rights for automated decision-making technology (ADMT) used in consequential decisions. Repeals and replaces SB 24-205's risk-management/impact-assessment framework (colorado_sb_205, now repealed) with a narrower disclosure-and-rights model: no duty of care against algorithmic discrimination, no deployer risk-management program, no annual impact assessment survive from the predecessor.
verification_note: Verified via web search and a fetch of the Colorado General Assembly's own SB26-189 bill page (https://leg.colorado.gov/bills/sb26-189) 2026-07-10 (Week 3), plus Norton Rose Fulbright's client summary. Governor Polis signed SB 26-189 on 2026-05-14. It repeals and reenacts SB 24-205 (colorado_sb_205, framework_version 1.0-sb24-205, now status=repealed with superseded_by=colorado_admt) with a substantively different scheme -- 'automated decision-making technology' (ADMT) and 'consequential decision' replace 'high-risk AI system'; the duty of care against algorithmic discrimination, the deployer risk-management-program requirement, and the annual impact-assessment requirement are all removed entirely, not carried forward in any form. Modeled as a new framework_id (colorado_admt) rather than a version bump of colorado_sb_205 per the repeal-and-replace test in docs/framework_authoring_guide.md section 7. EFFECTIVE DATE RESOLVED 2026-07-10 (Week 4 parallel cleanup): the apparent 2026-08-12 vs 2027-01-01 conflict is not a split between two different obligation categories (unlike NYC LL144's genuine enactment-vs-enforcement gap) -- it is Colorado's DEFAULT effective date (bills without a safety clause take effect 90 days after adjournment, which was 2026-08-12 for the session that adjourned 2026-05-13) versus the effective date SB 26-189's own text specifically designates, which overrides that default. Multiple sources (including law-firm analyses citing the bill directly) confirm SB 26-189 was drafted with its own effective-date clause setting 2027-01-01, so the 2026-08-12 default never applies to it. effective_from=2027-01-01 is now a single, resolved date, not a placeholder pending further review. AS OF TODAY (2026-07-10), Colorado still has no comprehensive AI/ADMT statute in force: SB 24-205 was repealed before ever taking effect, and SB 26-189 does not bind until 2027-01-01 -- a real, verified finding, not a gap in this dataset. NEEDS_OWNER_REVIEW (remaining, narrower than before): specific C.R.S. section citations were not confirmed against the primary enrolled text (too large to fetch directly this session) and are recorded generically pending that review; this does not affect the effective-date finding above. Sources: Colorado General Assembly, SB26-189 bill page (https://leg.colorado.gov/bills/sb26-189); Norton Rose Fulbright, 'Colorado enacts revised AI law' (https://www.nortonrosefulbright.com/en-us/knowledge/publications/18733d31/colorado-enacts-revised-ai-law); additional 2026-07-10 web search confirming SB 26-189's self-specified 2027-01-01 effective date overrides the state's default 2026-08-12 date.

covered_admt_consequential_decision.name: Covered ADMT Used in a Consequential Decision

criteria.summary: Automated decision-making technology (ADMT) -- technology that processes personal data and uses computation to generate output (predictions, recommendations, classifications, rankings, or scores) that is used to make, guide, or assist a consequential decision -- concerning an individual's access to, eligibility for, or compensation related to education, employment, housing, financial or lending services, insurance, health care services, or essential government services and public benefits.

consequential_decision_categories.essential_government_service: Essential government services and public benefits

criteria.summary: The system is not ADMT as defined (does not process personal data to generate a qualifying output), or its output is not used to make, guide, or assist a consequential decision in a listed category.
